coredata.dc:description: Development of biomass-based nanozyme sensors is an important measure to improve the catalytic performance of nanozymes and achieve efficient resource utilization. In this study, rice straw is used as raw material and modified by MgCl2 to synthesize biomass carbon dots nanozyme (Mg,Cl-rsCDs) with peroxidase activity. The oxygenous groups inherent in rice straw play a positive role in improving the catalytic activity. Compared with natural enzymes, Mg,Cl-rsCDs have the advantages of stronger stability, wider applicable temperature range and better catalytic kinetic behavior. The active sites and enzyme-like catalytic mechanism of Mg,Cl-rsCDs are studied by density functional theory and free radical recognition techniques. We consider that the modification of MgCl2 changes the electroneutrality of the original carbon skeleton and promotes the synergistic effect of functional groups to generate active sites. Relying on the peroxidase activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, a biomass nanozyme-natural enzyme cascade catalytic system is constructed and used to detect the content of xanthine in human urine. The system has excellent linear range (0.2–80 μM), low detection limit (0.08 μM) and satisfactory reproducibility (RSD = 2.3–4.8%). This work provides a new strategy for production of biomass nanozymes and deep utilization of straw, which is of great significance for biosensing and resource engineering.

head.ce:title: Rice straw-derived carbon based nanozyme sensor: Application of identifying human urine xanthine content and study of active sites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization
Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of biomass-based nanozyme sensors is an important measure to improve the catalytic performance of nanozymes and achieve efficient resource utilization. In this study, rice straw is used as raw material and modified by MgCl2 to synthesize biomass carbon dots nanozyme (Mg,Cl-rsCDs) with peroxidase activity. The oxygenous groups inherent in rice straw play a positive role in improving the catalytic activity. Compared with natural enzymes, Mg,Cl-rsCDs have the advantages of stronger stability, wider applicable temperature range and better catalytic kinetic behavior. The active sites and enzyme-like catalytic mechanism of Mg,Cl-rsCDs are studied by density functional theory and free radical recognition techniques. We consider that the modification of MgCl2 changes the electroneutrality of the original carbon skeleton and promotes the synergistic effect of functional groups to generate active sites. Relying on the peroxidase activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, a biomass nanozyme-natural enzyme cascade catalytic system is constructed and used to detect the content of xanthine in human urine. The system has excellent linear range (0.2–80 μM), low detection limit (0.08 μM) and satisfactory reproducibility (RSD = 2.3–4.8%). This work provides a new strategy for production of biomass nanozymes and deep utilization of straw, which is of great significance for biosensing and resource engineering.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes are a class of nanomaterials that can mimic the catalytic function of natural enzymes and have high structural stability [1,2]. After recent years of research, nanozymes have gradually caught up with and surpassed natural enzymes in terms of preparation strategies, action conditions and functions [3]. By generating specific substrates in catalytic reactions, nanozymes are made into many types of biosensors [4]. Among them, carbon based nanozymes have the advantages of high stability and large specific surface area, making them have strong application potential [5,6]. Among the series of carbon based nanozymes, carbon dots nanozymes (CDsN) have received widespread attention in the field of life sensing analysis due to their advantages in simple synthesis, excellent water solubility, etc [7,8]. The raw materials for the conventional synthesis of CDsN are divided into solid carbon materials such as carbon black and nanodiamond, or small organic molecules such as o-phenylenediamine and citric acid [9–11]. In a study, Wang et al. [12]. proposed the preparation of CDsN using polyethylenimine and hemin as raw materials. Although this material has decent catalytic performance, the carbon source is expensive and highly toxic, so it does not have advantages in terms of economy and environmental protection. To solve the above problems, the unique advantages of using biomass as the carbon precursor to prepare CDsN have gradually entered researchers' field of vision. In addition, modification has been considered to be one of the important means to improve the enzyme-like catalytic activity of CDsN [13,14]. Rational modification of CDsN may also provide sufficient potential for its environmental stability and recyclability [15]. At present, CDsN modified with iron, manganese, cobalt and other elements have been confirmed to obtain enzyme-like catalytic activity [16]. The modifying elements are connected to the graphite-like structure of CDsN in the form of coordination bonds or covalent bonds, which can effectively change the structure and chemical properties of CDsN [17]. The reason is that the introduction of heteroatoms into the carbon nucleus forms new energy levels or adjusts the initial bandgap, which changes the charge distribution on the surface of CDsN and creates new catalytically active sites [18]. Thus, exploring different modifiers to modify CDsN is of great significance for realizing the diversification and multi-functionalization of CDsN.

The development of green and efficient treatment technology is an important means to solve the annual increase in the amount of biomass waste from crops [19]. Among them, straw has the advantages of renewable and easily degradable is considered a promising biomass raw material for application in the fields of nanomaterials, environmental engineering and biotechnology [20–22]. At present, straw is mainly used in the paper industry, building materials and other fields [23,24]. Although certain economic benefits have been produced, factors such as low product prices and rising labor costs have restricted the further development of the straw industry [25]. Cellulose carbohydrates are the main component of straw, followed by a small amount of fat and protein [26]. The rich composition of straw gives it sufficient potential to be converted into excellent performance and environment-friendly carbon based nanomaterials in a variety of ways.

In related reports, MgCl2 as an inexpensive and environmental-friendly modifier has also been used in the preparation of biomass carbon [27]. The defect degree of carbon materials modified by MgCl2 is significantly improved [28]. In addition, Mg-doped CDs have been shown to have considerable biocompatibility and Cl doped in CDs helps to modulate their surface energy traps [29,30]. We believe that the above advantages are of great significance for the development of high-performance and economical biomass enzyme-like catalytic materials. Herein, we first proposed to prepare Mg,Cl-rsCDs by hydrothermal method with rice straw as the carbon precursor and MgCl2 as the modifier. Interestingly, Mg,Cl-rsCDs exhibited strong peroxidase activity. The possible active sites and catalytic mechanism of Mg,Cl-rsCDs were analyzed based on density functional theory (DFT).To evaluate the use-value of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, we applied them to the sensing of xanthine content. Xanthine is one of the intermediate compounds metabolized by purine nucleotides and deoxynucleotides in organisms [31]. The content of xanthine in body fluid is closely related to the occurrence of certain diseases, such as gout, uremia, renal failure, et al [32]. Xanthine can generate H2O2 and uric acid under the catalysis of xanthine oxidase (XOD). According to this principle, we used the chromogenic substrate after oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as the sensing signal to construct a cascade reaction sensing platform based on Mg,Cl-rsCDs-XOD and apply it to the detection of xanthine in urine samples (Scheme 1 ).

The morphology and structure of materials were characterized by the JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The X'Pert3 powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was used to record the crystal structure related parameters (PANalytical, Eindhoven, Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested by Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+ with monochromatic Al Kα as the X-ray source (PE, Minnesota, USA). FT-IR360 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR) was used to analyze the composition and surface functional groups of materials in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained on the F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The UV–Vis absorption spectra involved in this work were recorded on the TU1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PERSEE, Beijing, China). ZetaPALS was used to obtain the zeta potential of the material (BIC, New York, USA). Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were obtained by micro ESR (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany).

3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, o-phenylenediamine, 2,2′-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate), xanthine, H2O2, isopropanol, 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, glucose, cholesterol, tryptophan, histidine, arginine and lysine were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai China). Uric acid, urea, sarcosine, benzoic anhydride, phenylhydrazine, 2-bromoacetophenone, NaCl, CaCl2, KCl, Na2SO4, Na2CO3, BaCl2, Mg(NO3)2, MgSO4, FeCl2, FeCl3, MnCl2, CuCl2, cysteine, glycine, leucine, MgCl2, acetic acid and NaAc were obtained from Rhawn (Shanghai China). XOD and superoxide dismutase were purchased from Yuanye (Shanghai China). Mature rice straw was gotten by the local market (Harbin, China). Adult urine samples were provided by the Northeast Forestry University Hospital. All reagents are of analytical grade, and no further purification is required. All experimental steps were performed with deionized water. Unless otherwise specified, the room temperature involved in this work was 23 °C.

Rice straw was dried in the blast dryer after washing with ethanol and water. The dried rice straw was made into powder by the pulverizer. The hydrothermal synthesis strategy was applied to prepare Mg,Cl-rsCDs. In a typical procedure, straw powder (200 mg) and anhydrous MgCl2 (200 mg) were added to 30 mL deionized water. The mixture was transferred to the stainless steel reactors lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and reacted at the temperature of 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was over, the mixture in the reactors was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min. The centrifuged supernatant was filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane and further purified by dialysis (3500 Da) for 48 h. The dialysate was freeze-dried to obtain a brown solid powder of Mg,Cl-rsCDs.
In addition, rice straw carbon dots (rsCDs) without MgCl2 modification were prepared under the same conditions.

The study of steady-state kinetics was carried out at 37 °C. In general, the peroxidase activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs was studied based on Michaelis-Menten enzyme kinetic theory. In the system containing HAc-NaAc buffer solution (340 μL, pH = 4, 0.1 M) and Mg,Cl-rsCDs (20 μL, 2 mg mL−1), TMB and H2O2 were used as catalytic reaction substrates, respectively. The change in absorbance of the catalytic systems under different substrate concentrations was detected. The initial reaction velocity was calculated according to Lambert-Beer law. By fitting the Michaelis-Menten kinetic Eq. (1) and the Lineweaver-Burk Eq. (2), the catalytic reaction kinetic parameters (Km, Vmax) of Mg,Cl-rsCDs are obtained, which are used to describe the pros and cons of the catalytic performance.

Where v is the initial rate of the catalytic reaction, Km represents the Michaelis constant, Vmax represents the maximum reaction rate and [S] is the substrate concentration.

5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as the capture agent, and the generation of ·OH was evaluated by ESR spectroscopy. Three groups of samples were incubated in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4, 0.1 M): (1) Mg,Cl-rsCDs (20 μL, 2 mg mL−1) + DMPO (20 μL, 0.1 M) + H2O2 (10 μL, 0.4 M); (2) DMPO (20 μL, 0.1 M) + H2O2 (10 μL, 0.4 M); (3) Mg,Cl-rsCDs (20 μL, 2 mg mL−1) + DMPO (20 μL, 0.1 M). After incubating for 7 min at room temperature, the three groups of samples were transferred to the quartz capillary for ESR analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical titration of oxygenous groups on Mg,Cl-rsCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The OH functional groups on Mg,Cl-rsCDs were titrated with benzoic anhydride (BA) [33]. BA (8.8 mmol) and Mg,Cl-rsCDs (60 mg) were dispersed in CHCl3 (20 mL). The mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 24 h under the atmosphere of N2. After that, the precipitate was filtered off and washed extensively until no BA molecules could be detected by UV–Vis spectroscopy in the supernatant. The washed precipitate was vacuum dried for more than 12 h. The brown solid was collected as Mg,Cl-rsCDs(BA). Similarly, the CO functional groups and COOH functional groups on Mg,Cl-rsCDs were titrated with phenylhydrazine (PH) and 2-bromoacetophenone (BrPE), respectively.

DFT calculations were performed on the Gaussian 09 program. The B3LYP/6-311G basis set was used to geometrically optimize the configuration and conformation of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, and the most stable structure and Cartesian coordinates (Å) of Mg,Cl-rsCDs were simulated accordingly. Then, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), Mulliken charges distribution (MC) and molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) of Mg,Cl-rsCDs were calculated.

H2O2 solutions (400 μL) of different concentrations were added to the mixed solution containing TMB (40 μL, 15 mM), Mg,Cl-rsCDs (20 μL, 2 mg mL−1). Then, these systems were diluted to 800 μL with HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4, 0.1 M). After 10 min of incubation at room temperature, the UV–vis spectrum and absorbance at 652 nm of these systems were measured immediately.

Xanthine solutions (400 μL) of different concentrations and XOD (10 μL, 2.5 UN mL−1) were diluted to 500 μL with PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4, 0.01 M). The reaction systems were placed in the constant temperature environment at 37 °C and incubated for 5 min. After the incubation is completed, TMB (40 μL, 15 mM), and Mg,Cl-rsCDs (20 μL, 2 mg mL−1) were added to these systems, and the volume were adjusted to 800 μL with HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4, 0.1 M). After incubating for 10 min at room temperature, the UV–vis spectrum and the absorbance at 652 nm of these systems were measured immediately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of xanthine in adult urine
ce:section.ce:para: The urine samples of three adults were used as actual samples to evaluate the application value of Mg,Cl-rsCDs and the accuracy of the established sensing platform. Firstly, urine samples were kept at 95 °C for 10 min. Then BaCl2 and Na2SO4 were added to the urine sample to precipitate out possible interfering substances. After that, the treated urine samples were centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was filtered with a microporous membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm. Finally, collected the filtrate and determined the concentration of xanthine in the urine samples according to the above steps.

Although many carbon based nanomaterials with peroxidase activity have been reported, most of the materials have disadvantages that cannot be ignored, such as raw materials polluting the environment, complex synthetic routes and uneconomical. Rice straw is considered to be an ideal raw material for the preparation of biomass carbon dot nanozymes due to its wide source, low price and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. Due to the increased degree of lignification of the plant cells of the rice straw at the mature stage, its crude fiber content is increased [34,35]. The differences in the content of various components of rice straw from different origins at the maturity stage are not particularly significant. Combined with the related work on carbohydrate carbonization, we briefly illustrate the possible mechanism of preparing Mg,Cl-rsCDs using mature rice straw as raw material and MgCl2 as doping reagent in Fig. 1
[36]. Cellulosic carbohydrates and lignin rely on hydrophilic OH and hydrophobic aromatic skeletons are firstly decomposed and dehydrated under the hydrothermal synthesis conditions of high temperature and high pressure to generate soluble compounds such as ketones, aldehydes, and organic acids [37,38]. After heating, these original products are transformed into primary aggregate clusters through reactions such as aromatization, polymerization, condensation, etc. Finally, these primary polymers are further carbonized in a supersaturated critical environment to form Mg,Cl-rsCDs. To confirm the role of MgCl2 in the formation of biomass CDsN, the catalytic performances of rsCDs modified with Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+ and Cu2+ were investigated, respectively (Fig. S1). The preparation process of rsCDs(Fe2+), rsCDs(Fe3+), rsCDs(Mn2+) and rsCDs(Cu2+) were the same as Mg,Cl-rsCDs except for modifiers. The result shows that the enzyme-like activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs is better than other materials. The catalytic activities of the remaining four materials are rsCDs(Mn2+), rsCDs(Fe3+), rsCDs(Cu2+) and rsCDs(Fe2+) in descending order. Therefore, the role of MgCl2 as the modifier is thought to change the electronic structure and internal properties of rice straw during the preparation process. Specifically, Mg2+ helps to avoid excessive consumption of carboxyl groups during the formation of primary aggregate clusters [39]. The Cl element modulates the surface electronic cloud of the formed CDs, resulting in a decrease in the zeta potential [40]. These provide Mg,Cl-rsCDs with potential functional groups to facilitate substrate binding and catalysis.

Based on preliminary experiments, it is found that Mg,Cl-rsCDs have catalytic activity similar to the natural enzyme. Specifically, TMB was used as the chromogenic substrate, and the catalytic ability of Mg,Cl-rsCDs was evaluated based on the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB by H2O2. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the appearance of the system containing Mg,Cl-rsCDs + TMB + H2O2 is dark blue. In contrast, the appearance of the two systems containing rsCDs + H2O2 + TMB and H2O2 + TMB are light blue, and the absorbance of the two systems at 652 nm is far less than the former. This result shows that Mg,Cl-rsCDs can catalyze the reaction between H2O2 and TMB, while unmodified rsCDs do not. In addition, the Mg,Cl-rsCDs + TMB system has no obvious characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm, indicating that Mg,Cl-rsCDs will not oxidize TMB alone. Therefore, it is judged that Mg,Cl-rsCDs have similar peroxidase activity. The same results are obtained with 2, 2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as chromogenic substrates (Fig. 2B). The appearance of the system containing Mg,Cl-rsCDs + ABTS + H2O2 is dark green and shows the characteristic peak of oxABTS at 419 nm of the spectrum. The system containing Mg,Cl-rsCDs + OPD + H2O2 appears yellow, and the 451 nm of the spectrum shows the characteristic peak of oxOPD. However, the molar absorption coefficient (ε) of TMB is higher than that of ABTS and OPD. Therefore, TMB was selected as the chromogenic substrate for this experiment for the sensitivity of subsequent work.

In this work, rice straw and MgCl2 were used as carbon raw material and modifier, respectively. Thus, mass ratio of rice straw to MgCl2, temperature, and time of hydrothermal reaction are the key factors that affect the catalytic ability of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. Fig. S2A shows that the processes of the reaction between H2O2 and TMB catalyzed by Mg,Cl-rsCDs accelerated as the relative proportion of MgCl2 in the reaction system increased. However, when the proportion of MgCl2 in the reaction system gradually increased and exceeded that of rice straw, the catalytic ability of Mg,Cl-rsCDs did not seem to be significantly improved. The effects of reaction temperature and time on the catalytic ability of Mg,Cl-rsCDs were studied according to the above method. When the temperature and time of the hydrothermal reaction were increased to 180 °C and 6 h respectively, continuing to increase the reaction temperature or prolong the reaction time could not effectively improve the catalytic ability of Mg,Cl-rsCDs (Fig. S2B, C).

The morphology and structure of Mg,Cl-rsCDs were studied by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2C, Mg,Cl-rsCDs showed the uniform spherical structure. In addition, according to the HRTEM in the inset of Fig. 2C, Mg,Cl-rsCDs are observed to have clear lattice fringes. The lattice spacing is shown to be about 0.208 nm, which is consistent with the graphite (100) structure [41]. According to further statistical analysis of 200 spherical particles, the particle size distribution of Mg,Cl-rsCDs were calculated to be in the range of 2–8 nm (Fig. S3), and the average particle size was about 4.6 nm. EDS spectrum also show that Mg,Cl-rsCDs contain C, O, Mg and Cl, confirming the successful doping of Mg and Cl elements (Fig. 2D). The material has excellent dispersibility in water, which provides the necessary functional basis for the application of Mg,Cl-rsCDs in the field of bioanalysis.

FT-IR was used to analysis the functional groups and bonding methods of Mg,Cl-rsCDs and rsCDs. As shown in Fig. 2E, Mg,Cl-rsCDs and rsCDs have obvious characteristic absorption peaks at 3440 cm−1, 2930 cm−1, 1630 cm−1, and 1420 cm−1 in the spectrum. These peaks are respectively composed of OH, CH, CO, CO stretching vibration. Test results indicate that the surface of this nanozyme has polar functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which ensure excellent water dispersibility. The spectrum of Mg,Cl-rsCDs has a characteristic absorption peak at 667 cm−1 caused by the stretching vibration of C-Cl compared with those of rsCDs, which indicates that the Cl element has been exogenously doped into the structure. The crystal structure of Mg,Cl-rsCDs was further checked by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2F, Mg,Cl-rsCDs form an ordered crystal structure. Comparing the spectra with the carbon standard card (PDF%22-1069) and the Mg2C3 standard card (PDF%01-1138) shows that the diffraction peak of Mg,Cl-rsCDs are basically the same as the standard cards. The composition and structure of Mg,Cl-rsCDs were further studied by XPS. According to Fig. 2G, there are C, O, Mg and Cl elements in Mg,Cl-rsCDs, and the content of each element is 51.17%, 20.59%, 18.4%, and 10.1%, respectively. Test results show that Mg,Cl-rsCDs are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen and contain a small amount of magnesium and chlorine. In detail, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s at around 285.3 eV can be deconvolved into five components. The five types of binding energy are 284.6 eV, 285.7 eV, 286.5 eV, 287.7 eV, and 289.4 eV (Fig. 2H), which are attributed to CC/CC, CO, CCl, CO, and COOH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2I, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s at about 532.9 eV consists of three different states: OC component at 531.8 eV, COH component at 533.5 eV, and COO component at 534.7 eV. The Cl 2p high-resolution XPS spectrum at around 199.6 eV can be decomposed into two peaks (Fig. 2J), corresponding to two Cl states, namely ClC (200.2 eV) and ClCO (201.9 eV). In addition, the obvious peak at 51.3 eV is attributed to Mg 2p (Fig. 2K), and the Mg KLL accompanying Mg 1s represents that the Mg element is encapsulated in the carbon nuclear (Fig. 2G) [42]. In addition, the XPS spectrum of rsCDs was further studied (Fig. S4A). Compared with the spectrum of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, the spectrum of rsCDs mainly contains characteristic peaks of C and O elements. Among them, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s is fitted to five characteristic peaks at 284.7, 285.6, 286.9, and 288.9 eV, representing CC/CC, CO, CO and COOH, respectively (Fig. S4B). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s consists of OC component at 532.3 eV, COH component at 533.4 eV and COO– component at 534.5 eV (Fig. S4C).

The optical properties of Mg,Cl-rsCDs were studied by UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra. The UV–Vis spectrum of Mg,Cl-rsCDs in the range of 200–700 nm was checked first. As shown in Fig. S5A, Mg,Cl-rsCDs have no obvious absorption peak in the visible region, but a clear absorption peak appears at 270 nm in the UV region. It is due to the π-π* transition of CC [43]. Fluorescence spectra of Mg,Cl-rsCDs show that the best excitation wavelength of this material is 356 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength is 460 nm. As shown the inset in Fig. S5A, the aqueous solution of Mg,Cl-rsCDs is light yellow under the irradiation of sunlight and bright blue under the irradiation of UV light with the wavelength of 365 nm. In addition, when the excitation wavelength range is 290-390 nm, the emission wavelength of Mg,Cl-rsCDs remains basically unchanged (Fig. S5B). It shows fluorescence behavior independent of excitation wavelength, which is related to the particle size and surface state of Mg,Cl-rsCDs [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the peroxidase activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The factors that affect the catalytic process of Mg,Cl-rsCDs are similar to horseradish peroxidase (HRP) depending on the system pH, temperature, nanozyme concentration and incubation time. Firstly, HAc-NaAc buffer solution was used to establish different pH systems (pH = 3–6.5) to study the influence of different pH environments on the catalytic activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. Fig. S6A shows that Mg,Cl-rsCDs have the best catalytic performance under the reaction conditions of pH = 4. As the pH increases, the catalytic activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs drops sharply. When the pH is increased to 5.5, the catalytic activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs is basically lost. The reason may be that H2O2 is easily decomposed when the pH value of the system is higher than 5, thereby reducing the amount of ·OH produced by the catalytic reaction [45]. Compared with the pH stability of HRP, it is also found that the optimum pH range of Mg,Cl-rsCDs and HRP are almost the same. Next, the influence of different ambient temperatures (5–65 °C) on the catalytic performance of Mg,Cl-rsCDs was studied. According to Fig. S6B, it is indicated that Mg,Cl-rsCDs can maintain decent catalytic activity in an environment of 5–45 °C. However, HRP has good catalytic ability only in the temperature range of 30–40 °C. Thus, Mg,Cl-rsCDs have a wider applicable temperature range, which breaks through the limitation that HRP action conditions are affected by temperature. Then, as the concentration of Mg,Cl-rsCDs gradually increases, the initial velocity of the reaction system gradually increases (Fig. S6C). However, the growth rate of the speed gradually slows down when the concentration of Mg,Cl-rsCDs is higher than that of 2 mg mL−1. Thus, considering the material utilization rate and the catalytic effect, the concentration of Mg,Cl-rsCDs is selected as 2 mg mL−1 for the next experiment. Since Fig. S6C describes the concentration at which Mg,Cl-rsCDs are added, the final concentration of Mg,Cl-rsCDs in the system is 0.05 mg mL−1 when 2 mg mL−1 of Mg,Cl-rsCDs are added. In addition, Fig. S6D describes how the absorbance of the three reaction systems varies with the reaction time. The Mg,Cl-rsCDs + TMB + H2O2 system reacts completely in about 10 min, much faster than TMB + H2O2 system. It is indicated that Mg,Cl-rsCDs have the powerful catalytic ability, which can complete the detection of H2O2 as the substrate within 10 min.

The catalytic performance of Mg,Cl-rsCDs is further described by steady-state kinetics. Fig. 3 A, B depict the relationship between the substrate concentration and the initial reaction rate when H2O2 and TMB were used as substrates. According to calculation, the relationship between the substrate concentration and the initial reaction rate conforms to the Michaelis-Menten equation. Among them, the Michaelis constant (Km) and the maximum reaction rate (Vmax) are two parameters of the Michaelis equation, which are used to describe the affinity of the nanozyme to the substrate and the maximum reaction rate, respectively. The smaller the Km value, the greater the affinity between nanozyme and substrate. The larger the value of Vmax, the stronger the catalytic performance of the nanozyme. Km and Vmax are calculated according to the Lineweaver-Burk equation obtained by coordinating the Michaelis-Menten equation (Fig. 3C, D). In addition, the kinetic parameters of Mg,Cl-rsCDs were compared with HRP and other nanozyme materials (Table S1). Inspired by previous researchers, the coefficient (w) of nanozyme concentration and the coefficient (Kw) that measures the molecular weight of the substrate catalyzed per second per unit mass of the nanozyme were introduced [10]. When H2O2 was used as the substrate, the Km of Mg,Cl-rsCDs is much lower than that of HRP, while it is close to that when using TMB as the substrate. Whether H2O2 or TMB was used as the substrate, the Vmax values of Mg,Cl-rsCDs are higher than that of HRP. Thus, considering the catalytic ability, cost, stability and other factors, we frankly consider that Mg,Cl-rsCDs can be used as the nanozyme material that replaces the function of HRP. Furthermore, compared with the kinetic parameters of other nanozymes, Mg,Cl-rsCDs have an advantage in the Km value with H2O2 as the substrate. The affinity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs for TMB is slightly inferior to PMCS but stronger than other nanozymes. At the same time, comparing the Vmax of different nanozymes also indicates that Mg,Cl-rsCDs have great catalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of free radicals in catalytic systems
ce:section.ce:para: To explain the catalytic mechanism of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, the type of catalytic process of the system was first tested. When H2O2 was used as the substrate, by changing the TMB concentration in the system, it was found that the resulting Lineweaver-Burk equations are almost parallel (Fig. 4 A). A similar result can be obtained when TMB was used as the substrate (Fig. 4B). It indicates that the catalytic process conforms to a typical ping-pong process.

Introducing different radical quenchers to study the species of radicals generated during catalysis. Isopropanol (IPA), superoxide dismutase (SOD) and histidine (His) were respectively used to scavenge ·OH, O2 ·- and 1O2 in the system. As shown in Fig. 4C, the catalytic ability of the system containing IPA is significantly weakened, while the catalytic ability of other systems is basically the same as that of the blank group. With the increase of IPA content, the peak at 652 nm in the UV–vis spectra of the system gradually decreases until it disappears (Fig. 4D). Thus, it is preliminarily judged that ·OH was generated in the catalytic process mediated by Mg,Cl-rsCDs. ESR test results also confirm this view. The system containing Mg,Cl-rsCDs + DMPO + H2O2 shows four characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 in the spectrum (Fig. 4E). This is a typical signal for the existence of ·OH. However, similar phenomena did not appear in the DMPO + H2O2 and Mg,Cl-rsCDs + DMPO systems. According to the above test results, it is determined that Mg,Cl-rsCDs activate H2O2 to generate ·OH, which further oxidize TMB to oxTMB. In addition, Cl element plays an important role in the peroxidase activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. It is reported that the modified Cl in CDs has the ability to promote the reduction of H2O2 to ·OH [46]. In this process, Cl is converted into active chlorine. The catalytic performance of rsCDs decorated with Mg(NO3)2 and MgSO4 were also verified, respectively. The result shows that the peroxidase activities of rsCDs(Mg(NO3)2) and rsCDs(MgSO4) prepared under the same conditions as Mg,Cl-rsCDs are not ideal (Fig. S7).

DFT calculations were used to further illustrate the existence of active sites and the catalytic mechanism of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. Based on the characterization data of element content and bonding form of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, combined with the B3LYP/6-311G basis set, the local surface structure of Mg,Cl-rsCDs was optimized. Fig. 5 A shows the optimized structure of the local surface of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. Table S2 describes the Cartesian coordinates (Å) of the atoms. Frontline molecular orbital theory was performed first. As shown in Fig. 5C, the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of Mg,Cl-rsCDs are mainly concentrated on the CO, COOH, COCl, Cl, Mg which are connected to or interact with the carbon nucleus, respectively. The relatively low ΔE (LUMO energy (ELUMO) − HOMO energy (EHOMO)) of the system indicates the easy transition of electrons in the molecule. Along this line, the calculated results of electron affinity (A), ionization potential (I), Mulliken electronegativity (χ), electron chemical potential (μ), absolute hardness (η), electrophilicity index (ω) are listed in Table S3. These parameters are important for understanding the surface molecular structure and catalytic performance of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. MEP systematically studies the ability of molecular interactions based on local electric potential. Fig. 5D shows that the blue negative potential regions are mainly located at CO, COOH and COCl on the Mg,Cl-rsCDs surface. These positions readily donate electrons to nucleophilic reactions, so it is thought that these positions may be potential active sites for Mg,Cl-rsCDs. The possible reason is that the introduction of the Cl element into the surface of Mg,Cl-rsCDs caused part of the electrons to affect the O atoms in the carbonyl group due to the induction effect and conjugation effect to reduce the electrostatic potential [47]. According to the previous conclusions, the catalytic process mediated by Mg,Cl-rsCDs generates ·OH. The Km is relatively low when H2O2 is used as the substrate. Therefore, it can be concluded that H2O2 firstly occurs Fenton reaction at the low MEP site of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. After that, ·OH oxidizes TMB to produce oxTMB. Chemical titration methodology was further used to study the role of oxygen-containing functional groups in catalytic reactions. BA, PH, and BrPE were used as specific inactivators for OH, CO and COOH, respectively. Fig. 6 A–C confirm that the purified Mg,Cl-rsCDs (BA), Mg,Cl-rsCDs (PH) and Mg,Cl-rsCDs (BrPE) do not contain free inactivator. The O1s high-resolution XPS spectrum analysis of Mg,Cl-rsCDs(BA), Mg,Cl-rsCDs(PH), and Mg,Cl-rsCDs(BrPE) confirm that the inactivator successfully acted on Mg,Cl-rsCDs (Fig. 6D). Compared with Mg,Cl-rsCDs, the content of COH in Mg,Cl-rsCDs(BA) decreases from 32.61% to 23.51%, the content of CO in Mg,Cl-rsCDs(PH) decreases from 47.15% to 29.54%, and the content of COO- in Mg,Cl-rsCDs(BrPE) decreases from 20.23% to 4.55%. The catalytic activities of Mg,Cl-rsCDs(BA), Mg,Cl-rsCDs(PH), and Mg,Cl-rsCDs(BrPE) were studied under the same system as Mg,Cl-rsCDs. It is found that the activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs(BA) is higher than that of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, but the catalytic activities of Mg,Cl-rsCDs(PH), and Mg,Cl-rsCDs(BrPE) are lower than that of Mg,Cl-rsCDs (Fig. 6E, F). These results suggest that oxygenous groups have the significant effect on the peroxidase-like activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. According to the report by Sun et al., CO on the surface of graphene quantum dots can be used as catalytically active sites, COOH functional groups can be used as substrate binding sites, and OH functional groups have the function of inhibiting activity, which also confirms the above conclusions [48].

In addition, the B3LYP/6-311G basis set was also used to further calculate the MC parameters (Fig. 5B and Table S4). Combined with the characterization data of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, it is speculated that Mg,Cl-rsCDs modified by MgCl2 are regulated by structural defects, resulting in changes in surface charge distribution [49]. By testing the zeta potentials of Mg,Cl-rsCDs and rsCDs, it is found that the surface of the former is negatively charged and the surface of the latter is nearly neutral (Fig. 4F). In the environment of pH = 4, Mg,Cl-rsCDs can attract TMB through electrostatic interaction. Based on the calculation result of MC, it is inferred that atoms with relatively strong negative charges in the molecule are the affinity sites of TMB. The reason for the charge point may be that the doped Mg element and Cl element destroy the electroneutrality of the sp2 hybrid carbon atoms [50]. Comparing the Raman spectra of Mg,Cl-rsCDs and rsCDs also indicate that the peak intensity ratios of the D band and G band (ID/IG) of the former and the latter are 1.01 and 0.95, respectively (Fig. 2L). It shows that the rsCDs modified with MgCl2 have more defect structures inside. This defect can promote the interfacial electron transfer between the catalyst and the reactants, thus favoring the catalytic activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. In general, we consider that the inherent carbon skeleton and abundant oxygen containing functional groups contained in the straw are used as a hotbed for synergistic interaction with the modifier, which accelerates the catalytic process on the surface of Mg,Cl-rsCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection results of H2O2 and xanthine by the sensing platform with Mg,Cl-rsCDs as the core
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the peroxidase activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, the H2O2 sensing ability of Mg,Cl-rsCDs under optimal reaction conditions is evaluated first. As shown in Fig. 7 A, different concentrations of H2O2 are catalyzed by Mg,Cl-rsCDs to oxidize TMB, causing the solutions to appear blue. As the concentration of H2O2 increases, the absorbance of the systems gradually increases and the color of solutions also deepens (the inset in Fig. 7A). The response curves of absorbance (652 nm) varying with H2O2 concentration are plotted in Fig. 7B. When the concentration of H2O2 is in the range of 0.1–80 μM, a great linear relationship can be established with the absorbance of the solution at 652 nm (R2 = 0.997). Thus, the detection method based on Mg,Cl-rsCDs design can complete the accurate sensing of the related life active substances with H2O2 as the substrate.

Xanthine can be oxidized by XOD to produce uric acid and H2O2. Thus, selecting Mg,Cl-rsCDs which are sensitive to H2O2 and combining them with XOD to construct a cascade catalytic sensing platform can quantitatively detect the content of xanthine. Fig. 7C shows that with the increase of the concentration of xanthine, the absorbance of the systems increases, and the color gradually changes from colorless to blue. The response curves of absorbance (652 nm) varying with xanthine concentration are plotted in Fig. 7D. When the concentration of xanthine is in the range of 0.2–80 μM, a great linear relationship can be established with the absorbance of the system at 652 nm. The linear regression equation is: Y = 0.0079X + 0.024 (R2 = 0.998), where Y is the absorbance of the system at 652 nm, and X is the concentration of xanthine. After calculation, the detection limit (LOD) of the established sensing platform for xanthine is as low as 0.08 μM (LOD = 3σ/k), showing the accurate recognition capabilities for xanthine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability testing of the sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of the system has further verified the practicality of the constructed nanozyme-natural enzyme cascade catalytic sensing platform. Common ions and compounds in urine were used as interfering substances (Na+, K+, Ca2+, SO4 2-, CO3 2–, glucose, cholesterol, uric acid, urea, glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), sarcosine (Sar), tryptophan (Trp), lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg), leucine (Leu)). The concentration of interference systems was 25 times that of xanthine. As shown in Fig. S8A, the interference systems have a very weak effect compared to the xanthine system.

By testing the peroxidase activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs after long-term storage, it is judged whether Mg,Cl-rsCDs have sufficient stability to be competent for xanthine detection tasks. Mg,Cl-rsCDs were transferred to sterile test tubes and stored at 4 °C for long-term storage. After that, Mg,Cl-rsCDs were used to test the same concentration of xanthine every other week to study the change in the catalytic ability of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. After five weeks of testing, the catalytic activity of Mg,Cl-rsCDs per week does not change much from the initial value (Fig. S8B). Test results indicate that Mg,Cl-rsCDs have excellent stability and can be stored for a long time under relatively mild conditions. This highlights the advantages of Mg,Cl-rsCDs compare to natural enzymes in storage and transportation.

The detection of xanthine in urine has important reference significance for the clinical diagnosis of hyperuricemia and other related diseases. The established sensing platform was used to directly detect the processed urine samples. Accuracy and sensitivity were studied by the standard addition method. After testing, the concentrations of xanthine in the urine samples of three volunteers were determined to be about 1 μM (Table 1 ). These detected values are very close to the previously reported values for xanthine in urine samples [32]. The recovery rate of the spiked samples is in the range of 95.5–103.2%, and the RSD (n = 3) of the entire test does not exceed ±5%. Thus, this sensing platform can detect the content of xanthine in human urine simply, sensitively and accurately.

In addition, the advanced nature of the method proposed in this work is judged by comparing the reported xanthine detection results of different sensor platforms. Table S5 shows that the detection limit of this work is lower than those of sensing platforms based on the electrochemical signal, fluorescent signal and a few colorimetric signal. This sensing platform also has a wide linear range, laying a foundation for further testing of other biological samples. Not only that, the recovery of the sensor platform proposed in this work is not inferior to other sensor platforms. Thus, combined with the inherent advantages of Mg,Cl-rsCDs, this sensor platform based on the nanozyme will be more practical.

In general, we have successfully synthesized Mg,Cl-rsCDs with peroxidase activity using rice straw. Studies have shown that Mg,Cl-rsCDs have obvious advantages compared with HRP and other peroxidase materials in terms of stability, action conditions and catalytic kinetics. Based on DFT, we discuss the existence of active sites on the surface of Mg,Cl-rsCDs. It is concluded that CO, COOH, COCl on the surface of Mg,Cl-rsCDs modified by MgCl2 can be used as potential active sites. Based on this, the constructed cascade catalytic sensing platform with Mg,Cl-rsCDs as the core has great recognition ability for xanthine, and can complete the detection task of xanthine content in human urine with satisfactory results. Furthermore, Mg,Cl-rsCDs are universal for constructing a series of peroxidase-based cascade catalytic sensing platforms to detect bioactive molecules such as glucose and uric acid in the future. This work provides a new strategy for deep utilization of straw and production of biomass nanozymes, which is of great significance for agricultural engineering and biosensing.

The process of collecting and using human urine samples follows all applicable international, national and/or institutional guidelines. All biological experiments are authorized by the Ethics Committee of Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital.
